1: Leviticus 13 is addressed to both Moses and Aaron — uniquely including Aaron in the address, which signals that what follows is priestly practice, not general legal instruction. The chapter belongs to the priestly ritual law corpus and reflects a world in which the priest is the authorized evaluator of boundary-crossing conditions. In Egypt the equivalent role was divided: the asu (physician) diagnosed and treated; the ashipu (exorcist-priest) addressed the spiritual dimension. Israel collapses both into the single priestly role, making the priest the sole arbiter of tzara'at status.
2: Three Hebrew terms cover the spectrum of skin changes: se'et (swelling/elevation), sapachat (scab/eruption), and baheret (bright spot/shiny patch). These are symptom-descriptors, not diagnoses — the priest's task is to determine whether any of these presentations constitutes tzara'at (the overarching condition requiring exclusion). Modern dermatologists have proposed that these presentations correspond to psoriasis plaques, seborrheic dermatitis, and vitiligo respectively, among other conditions. What they share is visible discoloration of the skin's surface.
3: The two decisive diagnostic criteria for a positive tzara'at diagnosis are: (1) hair within the mark has turned white, and (2) the surface of the mark appears depressed (deeper than the surrounding skin). These criteria require close physical examination — the priest must look carefully at both hair color and surface topology. The white-hair criterion likely targets inflammatory conditions that affect hair follicles; the depth criterion targets conditions with tissue breakdown or thickening. Neither criterion by itself is sufficient — both are required for a positive first-visit diagnosis.
4: If the bright spot is white but the hair has NOT turned white and the patch is NOT depressed, the person is quarantined for seven days rather than immediately declared unclean. The seven-day quarantine period is structurally significant: it mirrors the creation week, applying a sabbatical waiting period to the diagnostic process. The patient does not go before any other authority during this week — the quarantine is both medical observation and a kind of ritual pause in which the community waits for the condition to declare itself definitively.
5: The first re-inspection on day seven: if the mark looks unchanged and has not spread, another seven days of quarantine is imposed. The spreading criterion — whether the condition is active and expanding — is the primary concern. Stable, non-spreading conditions are less alarming than actively spreading ones because they suggest a contained, bounded process. The boundary-integrity logic of the purity system is directly encoded in this diagnostic procedure: a condition that crosses and expands its own boundaries is more threatening than one that stays within fixed limits.
6: After the fourteen-day (two-week) quarantine, if the condition has faded and not spread, the priest declares it a mispachat (a harmless eruption, a mere rash) and pronounces the person clean. The progression from maximal concern (initial presentation) to re-evaluation (day 7) to further re-evaluation (day 14) to declaration represents a graduated diagnostic process that protects against both under-diagnosing (missing real tzara'at) and over-diagnosing (excluding a healthy person from the community). The two-stage quarantine provides a natural history of the condition.
7: If the condition spreads after the priest has cleared it for cleansing, the person must return for re-examination. The possibility of a condition worsening after initial clearance reflects awareness that skin conditions can be dynamic — remitting and relapsing. The requirement to return if symptoms worsen creates a surveillance mechanism: people are responsible for monitoring their own conditions and re-presenting if they deteriorate. This self-reporting requirement extends the priestly diagnostic system into the individual's daily life.
8: Spreading at the second examination = unclean. The diagnostic system ultimately rests on the spreading criterion: a condition that is actively expanding its boundaries is the defining marker of tzara'at. The priest's declaration (vetimme'o ha-kohen tzara'at hi = 'the priest shall declare him unclean: it is tzara'at') is a formal legal verdict, not merely a medical opinion. The priest's word has social-legal force: the declared person is excluded from the community until re-evaluated and cleared.
9: When skin disease is clearly present, the person is brought to the priest — this is not self-diagnosis but community-mediated process. The passive construction ('he shall be brought') implies social oversight: the community, not just the individual, has a role in presenting cases for priestly evaluation. In close-quarters living (the Israelite camp in the wilderness, or village communities in Canaan), skin disease affecting one person's suitability for participation in worship and community life was a community concern, not merely a personal medical matter.
10: Chronic/established skin disease presents with all three major signs: white swelling, white hair in the swelling, and raw flesh (basar chai = living flesh, raw exposed tissue). The presence of raw flesh is the most significant indicator — it signals active tissue breakdown with exposed internal tissue. In the purity system's anthropology, the integrity of the skin as boundary is fundamental: the skin separates inside from outside, clean from potentially contaminating. Raw exposed flesh is a categorical boundary violation — the inside is outside.
11: When all three signs are present together (chronic presentation with raw flesh), no quarantine is needed: the priest declares the person immediately unclean. The 'do not isolate him' instruction means there is no need for the diagnostic observation period — the condition is self-evidently tzara'at. The immediate declaration without quarantine reflects diagnostic confidence: when the signs are unambiguous, the system does not require the graduated evaluation process used for ambiguous presentations. Severity and clarity of signs shortens, not lengthens, the process.
12: The paradoxical clean-whole-body-coverage rule: if the disease has spread to cover the entire body 'from head to foot, everywhere the priest looks,' the priest declares the person clean. This is counterintuitive from a modern medical standpoint — more extensive disease should be more serious — but it follows an internal logic of the purity system. When the entire surface has turned white (as in some presentations of vitiligo or psoriasis covering the whole body), there is no visible boundary between 'normal' and 'affected': the distinction that creates impurity has been erased by totality.
13: The whole-body-coverage rule ('since it has all turned white, he is clean') reflects a boundary-theory of purity: impurity arises where there is a problematic transition between states, not in the state itself. A uniformly white-covered person has no boundary between affected and unaffected — the problematic margin is gone. This same logic appears elsewhere in the purity system: a whole animal is clean; a hybrid animal (two different kinds) is unclean. Categorical purity/impurity exists at boundaries and transitions, not within uniform states.
14: The exception immediately follows: if raw flesh appears at any point on the otherwise uniformly covered body, the person is unclean. The raw flesh (basar chai) creates a new visible boundary — between the white skin and the exposed raw area — that reintroduces the problematic transition. The clean-whole-coverage status is immediately revoked by the appearance of even a small patch of raw tissue. The diagnostic system is dynamic: conditions change, and re-evaluation is triggered by any change in the boundary situation.
15: The priest examines the raw flesh and declares unclean. The raw flesh takes priority over the whole-body-coverage status — the raw-flesh criterion overrides the whole-coverage exception. In the hierarchy of diagnostic criteria, raw exposed tissue is the most severe indicator, more significant than surface discoloration alone. The clinical picture makes sense: raw exposed tissue in a skin condition suggests active ulceration or erosion, which would transmit pathogens in ways that surface discoloration might not.
16: If the raw flesh heals (turns white again), the person returns to the priest for re-evaluation. The dynamic, reversible character of tzara'at status is explicit: the condition can improve, and improvement triggers a new examination that may result in a clean declaration. The purity system is not a one-way ratchet toward increasing impurity — it provides for restoration to clean status when the condition resolves. This reversibility is both medically realistic and theologically significant: exclusion is not permanent punishment but a provisional status tied to the condition.
17: The priest examines the healed area: if the mark has turned white, the person is declared clean. The same visual criterion (white coverage, no raw flesh) that was diagnostically significant on first presentation is now the criterion for declaring clean after healing. The symmetry of the diagnostic criteria in both directions (clean → unclean upon appearance; unclean → clean upon resolution) provides a coherent framework: the priest applies the same standards consistently regardless of which direction the evaluation is moving.
18: A second etiological category: skin disease arising at the site of a healed boil (shechin). The shechin (boil) appears in the plague narratives (Ex 9:9-11, the sixth plague on Egypt), in Job's affliction (Job 2:7), and in Hezekiah's potentially fatal illness (Isa 38:21, 2 Kgs 20:7 treated with a fig poultice). The instruction to evaluate a post-boil site for tzara'at reflects awareness that secondary skin changes at wound or infection sites can mimic or develop into more serious conditions. Post-inflammatory hypopigmentation or hyperpigmentation at healed wound sites is common in tropical and subtropical climates.
19: The post-boil presentation: a white swelling (se'et levana) or reddish-white bright spot (baheret levana admedmet) at the healed boil site. The reddish-white (admamdam) qualifier appears for the first time here — a pink or rose-tinted discoloration at the scar site. The color is significant: purely white marks differ from reddish-white ones in their diagnostic implications. The distinction between white and reddish-white anticipates the graduated color diagnostic in the burn section (vv. 24-28) and the head-disease section (vv. 29-37).
20: Diagnostic criteria for the post-boil site: if the mark appears deeper than the surrounding skin AND the hair has turned white → the priest shall pronounce it a skin disease arising from the boil. The same two criteria as the initial diagnosis (vv. 3) apply here: depth + white hair = tzara'at. The post-boil context does not change the diagnostic standards — the priestly system is consistent. The disease at a boil site is evaluated by the same criteria as primary skin disease, which prevents the boil-origin from being used as an explanation to dismiss legitimate tzara'at.
21: If the post-boil mark has no white hair, is not depressed, and has faded → quarantine seven days. The same graduated response for ambiguous presentations as in vv. 4-5: when the clear diagnostic criteria are absent, a seven-day quarantine provides time for the condition to declare itself. The parallel structure of the boil-derived and primary tzara'at diagnostic procedures shows systematic medical-priestly procedure: the etiological origin of the skin change does not alter the evaluation protocol.
22: If the mark spreads during quarantine → unclean; it is a netek (diseased mark / spreading condition). The spreading criterion remains primary across all etiological categories. Whether the skin change arose spontaneously, from a boil, from a burn, or from the scalp, the defining feature that elevates a skin change to the level requiring exclusion is active spreading. The consistency of this criterion across all variants reflects a coherent medical-theological logic: the threatening quality is active boundary-crossing, not origin.
23: If the bright spot stays in place and does not spread → it is the scar of the boil (tzeleqet ha-shechin) and the person is declared clean. The scar explanation (the mark is simply a healed remnant of the boil) is the benign interpretation: the discoloration is historical (a record of the healed wound) rather than active (an ongoing disease process). The priest's ability to provide a scar-origin explanation for a stable, non-spreading mark reflects sophisticated clinical reasoning: not every skin change is disease; some are the normal traces of healed injury.
24: The third etiological category: skin disease arising from a burn scar (mikevat esh). Burns were a common injury in ancient domestic life — cooking fires, oil lamps, pottery kilns, and metalworking all created burn risks. Post-burn skin changes (hypopigmentation, hyperpigmentation, keloid scarring) are common and can visually resemble the presentations that concern the priestly diagnostic system. The specific mention of burns as a separate category alongside boils shows the legislators' awareness of different wound-healing trajectories.
25: Positive diagnosis at a burn site: white hair in the bright spot AND the mark appears deeper than surrounding skin → tzara'at arising from the burn. The criteria are identical to those for primary tzara'at (v. 3) and post-boil tzara'at (v. 20). The consistency across all three etiological categories (primary, post-boil, post-burn) reflects the system's orientation toward the current presentation rather than the historical cause: the origin of the mark is noted for context, but the diagnostic decision is made on the same visual criteria regardless of origin.
26: No white hair, not deeper, and faded → quarantine for seven days. The same seven-day quarantine for ambiguous post-burn presentations as for ambiguous primary presentations. The graduated evaluation procedure (quarantine → re-inspect → declare or extend quarantine) is applied consistently. The burn context adds one medical nuance: burn sites commonly hypopigment (lighten) during healing, producing white-ish marks that are normal post-burn appearance rather than tzara'at. The quarantine period distinguishes evolving post-burn hypopigmentation (fading toward normal) from developing tzara'at (persistent or spreading).
27: Day-seven re-inspection for post-burn site: if it has spread → declare unclean. The same outcome as in the primary and post-boil cases: spreading = tzara'at. The burn section (vv. 24-28) is the most compressed of the three etiological categories — its parallel structure with the boil section (vv. 18-23) allows the parallel procedure to be stated more concisely. The two cases together (boil and burn) establish that the same priestly diagnostic system applies to secondary-onset skin changes regardless of the triggering injury type.
28: If the bright spot has stayed in place, has not spread, and has faded → it is the swelling from the burn, and the person is declared clean. The burn-scar explanation (like the boil-scar explanation in v. 23) provides the benign interpretation for a stable, fading post-burn mark. The priest's dual role in this system is both diagnostic and socially protective: diagnosing tzara'at protects the community from potentially contagious conditions; clearing stable post-wound marks protects individuals from unnecessary exclusion.
29: The fourth major category: skin disease of the head (rosh) or chin (zaqan). The chin/beard region is specified because it is where male Israelites wear beards, making beard-area skin conditions a distinct diagnostic context. Hair growth in the affected area affects both the visual presentation of the mark and the diagnostic criteria: head/face hair differs from body hair in color and growth pattern. The distinct criteria for head/chin disease (vv. 30-37) reflect awareness that the same underlying condition presents differently in bearded/scalp areas.
30: Scalp or beard disease diagnosis: if deeper than surrounding skin AND containing thin yellow (tza'hur) hair → pronounce unclean. This is a nefeq (scale disease, ringworm, tinea capitis or barbae). Notably, the hair criterion shifts from white (body disease) to yellow-thin: this reflects the different visual presentation of fungal scalp infections, which characteristically produce scaly patches with thin, broken, yellowish hairs due to fungal invasion of the hair shaft. The diagnostic criteria for scalp disease are thus adapted to the distinctive presentation of fungal (rather than inflammatory) conditions.
31: Ambiguous scalp/beard disease: no yellow hair, not deeper than surrounding skin → quarantine seven days (no black hair in it). The absence of black hair in the mark is specified because the appearance of normal black (or dark) hair regrowth within the mark during quarantine is a sign of healing. The scalp diagnostic system thus monitors hair color within the mark — a form of follicular health assessment. Yellow/thin = fungal disease active; black/normal = disease resolving or absent.
32: Day-seven re-inspection of the scalp/beard mark: if the itch has not spread AND no yellow hair AND does not appear deeper — quarantine another seven days. The second seven-day quarantine for scalp conditions follows the same pattern as for body conditions. The patient is told to shave the surrounding area (v. 33) but NOT the itch itself, so that the priest can continue to monitor the hair within the mark. Shaving the surround creates a 'cleared field' for observing the affected area without the distraction of surrounding hair.
33: The instruction to shave the surrounding area but not the itch itself is a practical diagnostic procedure: removing surrounding hair allows clearer visual access to the affected patch, enabling more accurate assessment of whether the itch is spreading into previously normal scalp. The preservation of hair within the itch allows continued monitoring of hair color changes (yellow → black indicating recovery). The combined shave-and-preserve instruction represents sophisticated clinical thinking about optimal visualization of the diagnostic field.
34: Day fourteen re-inspection: if the itch has not spread AND does not appear deeper than the skin → clean. The person washes their clothes (standard cleansing procedure after time in quarantine) and is declared clean. The washing-of-clothes requirement frames the return to clean status as a transition requiring ritual action, not merely a verbal declaration. The body and its covering (clothes) both participate in the transition from impure to pure status, reflecting the holistic purity worldview in which impurity extends from person to garments.
35: Post-cleansing spread → re-examination required. As in the body-condition cases (vv. 7-8), a condition can worsen after the priest has initially cleared it. The surveillance obligation (return if symptoms worsen) extends indefinitely after the initial clean declaration. The spread of a previously cleared scalp condition forces re-examination — the initial clean verdict is not permanent if the condition becomes active again. This dynamic surveillance model reflects realistic awareness that dermatological conditions are not always cured by the first resolution of symptoms.
36: Spread at re-examination after initial clearing: if the itch has spread, the priest does not need to look for yellow hair — the person is unclean regardless. The spread criterion overrides all other diagnostic considerations for re-examination cases: when a condition is clearly active and spreading, the hair-color assessment becomes redundant. The prioritization of spread over hair color in this context reflects a risk hierarchy: spreading (the community-threatening feature) trumps color (a feature primarily useful in ambiguous initial presentations).
37: But if the itch appears unchanged AND black hair has grown in it → the itch is healed, and the priest declares the person clean. The appearance of black hair within the previously affected patch is a positive prognostic sign — the hair follicles have recovered and are producing normal, pigmented hair. Black hair regrowth within a previously thinned, yellow-haired patch is a visible indicator of follicular health recovery. The priest's ability to assess healing through hair regrowth color represents a sophisticated natural-history observation.
38: A special category: bright white spots on the skin of a man or woman. This presentation is described separately from tzara'at and is diagnosed as clean. The distinction between this and tzara'at presentations is subtle — the key difference is that these spots are kehot (dull white, not shining/bright white) in v. 39, suggesting a different appearance from the baheret (bright/shining spot) that characterizes tzara'at. Vitiligo produces exactly this kind of flat, depigmented patch without surface change — the mkt-original term bohaq (v. 39) likely refers to vitiligo or other benign hypopigmentation conditions.
39: The priest examines the dull white spots: if they are kehot (dull, faded, non-shining) → it is a harmless eruption (bohaq) and the person is clean. The contrast between a baheret levana (shining white spot = tzara'at) and bohaq keheh (dull white eruption = clean) requires the priest to distinguish degrees of whiteness by visual inspection. This diagnostic distinction between bright and dull white reflects awareness that not all skin depigmentation is disease: the visual quality of the discoloration, not merely its presence, determines the diagnosis.
40: Ordinary male-pattern baldness is explicitly declared clean: 'He is bald but he is clean.' The clarification is socially necessary because baldness involves hair loss — the same symptom that in a specifically textured scalp mark would indicate disease. The clean verdict for ordinary baldness prevents the over-application of the tzara'at diagnostic system to normal age-related or hereditary hair loss. The legal principle is contextual: hair loss matters as a symptom only within the context of a skin mark; isolated hair loss without accompanying skin change is not pathological in the system's terms.
41: Front-baldness (receding hairline, frontal hair loss — gibbeach) is also clean. The two baldness terms — qereach (crown/back baldness) in v. 40 and gibbeach (front baldness) in v. 41 — correspond to what modern medicine calls vertex alopecia and frontal alopecia. Both are common in men and are explicitly categorized as non-pathological. The inclusion of both specific types of baldness reflects careful legal drafting: the law specifies all common presentations of male baldness so that none of them can be mistakenly applied to the tzara'at diagnostic system.
42: Exception: if a reddish-white (admedmet levana) mark is present on the bald area, it may be tzara'at. The reddish-white discoloration on an otherwise naturally bald scalp triggers the diagnostic system. The bald area serves as a 'cleared field' analogous to the shaved area in v. 33 — the absence of covering hair on the bald scalp makes any skin change directly visible. The reddish-white coloration is the same term used for post-boil and post-burn marks (vv. 19, 24), suggesting it describes an inflamed, active lesion rather than a stable, faded one.
43: The priest examines the bald-scalp mark: if the reddish-white swelling looks like tzara'at as it would appear anywhere else on the body → the person has tzara'at of the bald scalp. The bald-scalp tzara'at is diagnosed by analogy with body tzara'at: the visual appearance must match the presentation that would be diagnostic elsewhere. The comparative criterion ('looks like tzara'at in the skin of the body') prevents both over-diagnosis (declaring any bald-scalp mark as tzara'at) and under-diagnosis (exempting bald-scalp marks from scrutiny).
44: The declaration: the person has tzara'at and the priest pronounces him unclean; his affliction is on his head. The specification 'on his head' in the formal declaration is important: it locates the condition for ongoing monitoring and distinguishes head-tzara'at (bald area) from body-tzara'at. The formal priestly declaration ('the priest shall pronounce him unclean') is the critical social act — it transforms a private medical condition into a public legal status with community-level implications.
45: The social obligations of the declared-tzara'at person reflect mourning gestures: torn clothing (begadim yihyu ferumim), disheveled/uncovered hair (rosh yihyeh para), covered upper lip (al shafam ya'ateh), and the public cry 'Unclean! Unclean!' (tameh tameh yiqra). All four actions are associated with mourning for the dead in the OT (cf. Ezek 24:17,22 for the uncovered head and lip in mourning). The declared-impure person is socially treated as belonging to the realm of death and loss — their condition places them at the boundary between life (covenant community) and not-life (exile from community).
46: The duration: 'he shall remain unclean as long as he has the disease' — the status is tied to the condition's persistence, not a fixed time period. He must live apart, badad yeshev (dwell alone), outside the camp. The outside-the-camp dwelling is the spatial exclusion corresponding to the social exclusion: the covenant community's spatial boundary (the camp) reflects its covenantal-ritual boundary. Living 'outside the camp' places the person in the same space as the burned remains of sin offerings (Lev 4:12,21) — the zone of removed impurity and completed sacrifice. The dwelling-alone requirement prevents the person from forming an alternative social unit while excluded from the main community.
47: The law extends to garments: tzara'at-like marks can appear on wool or linen garments. The extension of the purity-boundary concept from persons to objects reflects the comprehensive holism of the Levitical system: impurity is not merely a personal status but a property that can transfer to and reside in objects. The two fibers specified — wool (tzemer) and linen (pishtim) — are the two primary textile materials of ancient Israel. Leather (or) is added in v. 48. These are also the materials of the tabernacle, making the fabric-purity laws directly relevant to cultic practice.
48: The specific textile structures covered: sheti (warp) and erev (woof/weft) of linen or wool, and leather or leather articles. The distinction between warp (lengthwise threads) and woof (crosswise threads) in woven textiles is relevant because some mold and mildew infections spread along thread-channels — the warp or woof direction of spread might indicate which structural element of the fabric is the primary host. The inclusion of all structural forms of fabric (woven and leather) ensures comprehensive coverage of the fabric materials in common use.
49: The diagnostic color for fabric disease: greenish (yaraqraq) or reddish (admedmet) in the garment. These colors correspond to common mold pigmentations: greenish coloration is characteristic of Cladosporium, Aspergillus, and Penicillium mold species; reddish-orange coloration appears in Neurospora and Fusarium species. The specification of these colors as the warning signs reflects empirical observation: in the ancient world where textiles were stored in conditions of variable humidity, experienced priests or handlers would recognize these characteristic discolorations as signs of biological growth requiring inspection.
50: The priest quarantines the afflicted garment or article for seven days. The seven-day quarantine applies to objects on the same principle as persons: the diagnostic period allows observation of whether the condition spreads or resolves. The parallel structure with the skin disease quarantine (same duration, same re-inspection logic) reflects the system's extension of the same conceptual framework from persons to objects. Priests in ancient Israel thus functioned not only as medical diagnosticians but as inspectors of textiles, leather goods, and (in ch. 14) buildings.
51: On the seventh day: if the mark has spread in the garment or any leather article → the mark is an active tzara'at in the fabric and the article is declared unclean. The spreading criterion applies to fabric exactly as it does to skin: an active, expanding discoloration that spreads through the textile structure indicates a live biological growth (mold/mildew/fungus) that is not self-limiting. The burning required (v. 52) for a spreading fabric mark is the most severe disposal method — more definitive than the outside-the-camp exclusion for persons.
52: Burning the afflicted garment or article: the most severe outcome in the fabric purity system. Unlike persons (who can be declared clean after healing), a garment that has spreading tzara'at is burned with fire. The burning (vesaraf et ha-beged) is the irreversible disposal of a contaminated object. The burning mirrors the burning of the carcasses of the priest's and community's sin offerings outside the camp (Lev 4:12,21): in both cases, the contamination-bearing material is completely destroyed by fire rather than being cleaned or repurposed.
53: If the mark has NOT spread after seven days, the priest orders the affected article to be washed and quarantined for another seven days. The two-stage process for fabric parallels the two-stage quarantine for ambiguous skin conditions (vv. 4-6): initial quarantine (day 7) → wash + second quarantine (day 14) → final determination. The washing step is significant: if washing removes the color, the mark may be a simple stain or surface deposit rather than a penetrating biological growth. The washing test distinguishes between surface contamination (removable) and structural contamination (not removable).
54: The command to wash and quarantine represents the fabric purity system's equivalent of the skin-disease 'wait and see' protocol. Washing a potentially mold-affected garment in ancient conditions (hand-washing with water and perhaps lye-based cleanser) could remove surface fungal growth while leaving penetrating fungal mycelium intact. The quarantine after washing allows time to observe whether the fungal growth re-emerges from within the fiber structure after the surface contamination is removed — a form of penetration testing.
55: After washing, if the mark has not changed appearance and has not spread → the article is unclean. The unchanged mark despite washing indicates the contamination is structural (embedded in the fiber) rather than superficial (a removable deposit). The additional assessment 'whether the spot has eaten into the back or front of the garment' evaluates the penetration depth: has the contamination reached the underside? Penetrating contamination is more severe than surface contamination and may require burning even without active spreading.
56: If the mark has faded after washing → the priest tears out the affected part from the garment, leather, warp, or woof. The surgical excision of the affected area is the moderate outcome between full burn (v. 52) and full cleaning (v. 58): when the contamination has responded to washing (faded) but not fully resolved, removing only the affected section salvages the rest of the article. This partial-destruction option reflects economic pragmatism: destroying an expensive garment entirely when only a section is affected would impose unnecessary economic hardship.
57: If the contamination reappears after section removal → burn the entire article. A spreading condition that returns after surgical section removal has demonstrated that the contamination cannot be contained by partial removal. The sequence (wash → section removal → reappearance) demonstrates a persistent, aggressive biological growth that requires complete destruction. The escalating response (quarantine → wash → section removal → total burn) mirrors modern protocols for persistent fungal contamination in textiles and food storage facilities.
58: If washing fully removes the mark → wash a second time and declare the article clean. The double-washing requirement for articles that have cleared after the first wash ensures that the cleaning is thorough before returning the article to use. The confirmation washing prevents premature re-introduction of articles whose contamination may have only superficially cleared. The requirement also has the practical effect of standardizing the cleaning protocol: every cleaned fabric article must be washed twice before being declared clean and returned to use.
59: The closing formula: 'This is the law (zot torat nega ha-tzara'at) for a skin disease mark on a garment of wool or linen, in the warp or woof, or on any leather article — to pronounce it clean or to declare it unclean.' The closing zot torat formula (identical to the closing of ch. 7, 11, 12, and appearing at the end of ch. 14 for the full tzara'at law) marks a literary-legal conclusion. The binary outcome — clean or unclean — is explicitly stated as the purpose of the entire elaborate inspection system: the system exists to produce a definitive, authoritative verdict.
